availability.p:
Published under a
Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.p:
Therefore that word contained in the bull "to subject" must be understood civilly and christianly: which beyond the above is proven from the common principles of law that any law or constitution ought to be extended or restricted: to the limits of the expressed reason: even if the words do not allow it as in L. adigere. §. quamuis. ff. de Iure patio and in l. cum pater, § dulcissimis. ff. de legatis 2o and in L. pater. §. fundum. ff. de lega. 3o and in c. cum dilecta de confir. utili. And in regula fi. de regulis Iuris. lib. 6 where it is said:
it is certain that he commits against the law: who embracing the words of the law: strives against the intention of the law. And always the mind of the prince must be examined, observed and followed even if it does not agree with the propriety of the words in the said § dulcissimis. For always the mind of the speaker must be considered rather than the voice. ff. de suppellecti. lega. L. labeo. Nor should the word be examined more than the will of the speaker. c. quae res pigno. obliga. possunt. l. ultima And
22. q. 5 c. humanae aures. and in l. scire leges: cum duabus sequen. ff. de legi. and it is noted in l. quamuis and there the gloss. finalis. ff. de in ius vocando Panormitanus and others in c. quia insulis de regularibus after John of Legnano in c. audientiam. de decimis. For words ought to serve the intention and not the intention serve the words: as has been proven above. And John of Legnano in c. ad audientiam de decimis And generally in the letters of the prince the mind must be considered: and the reason upon which his commands rest as in c. si propter tua debita and there the doctors.
Ancharanus in the repetition of the same chapter de Rescriptis libro 6. and in c. 2, eo titulo and c. ultimo de concessione praebendae libro 6, and in c. ex multiplici de decimis. Archidiaconnus. 25. q. 2. c. Seruatis. says, that where it is evident concerning the intention of the pope that intention must be followed entirely. Likewise, that the words of the pope enunciating the intention from of old determine and prove. 25. q. 2. § quod autem Baldus reports and confirms these things in the proem of the book of feuds § aliqua in principio.

Now the mind of the pontiff was that those peoples should be led to Christ. But this can in no way be done by war which rather generates hatred than love of our religion. Therefore by gentleness, charity, holy customs and the word of God the Indians must be led to the faith not by war concerning which the pontiff never dreamed (as we have often said) and the kings themselves understood that those peoples were to be subjected not by war but by the word of God as commanded to them by the pontiff as has been most clearly proven above. With these agrees that in odious matters words are to be strictly interpreted while favorable matters are to be extended and expanded.
Hatreds should be restricted and favors should be amplified c. odia de regulis juris lib. 6. But war which Homer writes is sent from hell is the most miserable and pestilential of all things that are under heaven: and completely incompatible with the life and teaching of Christ unless when unavoidable necessity compels it. But to invite pagans to the faith by the word of God and evangelical customs is most holy and truly Christian and suitable for extending and exalting the glory of Christ.

Let us therefore restrict war, the pestilence of body and soul: and let us embrace evangelical preaching and the sword of the divine word more efficacious than all human arms. To these is added that even those things which regularly ought to be restricted such as penalties: are extended / If it concerns public utility according to Bartolus in l. quemadmodum. C. de agricol. et censib. but these things pertain to the public salvation of those peoples: that they should be led to the faith not by war deadly to mortals: but by the word of God and to the rule of the kings of Spain. For by war it will rather happen that they hate the faith than that they embrace it. Then I believe no utility to be more public: than that which besides the tranquility of so many provinces / generates the salvation of so many myriads of mortals. For the soul must be preferred to all things. as in
c. cum infirmitas. de penitentiis et remissionibus and in c. praecipimus. 12. q. 1

Let us therefore restrict that word "to subject": so that it is understood concerning the subjection which will be born from the preaching of the divine word: Gentle and mild: in which sense that word must be accepted even if its proper signification were opposed. Especially since there exists the bull of Paul III, Roman pontiff which expressly prohibits these detestable wars which are waged against the Indians under the pretext of religion: commanding: that they should be led to the faith not by warlike terror but by the word of God and holy customs. The content of this bull given in the year 1537 I have recounted above in the chapter.
